law: Among other things, you can tell keychain services that every time it tries to read the item, it should first seek the user’s permission—for example, by authenticating the user biometrically with Face ID or Touch ID.
To access keychain items in general, you use keychain services within the Security framework.
Without this key, the system won’t allow your app to use Face ID.
The value for this key is a string that the system presents to the user the first time your app attempts to use Face ID.
The string should clearly explain why your app needs access to this authentication mechanism.
The system doesn’t require a comparable usage description for Touch ID.
Keychain services automatically makes use of the LocalAuthentication framework, and in particular uses an instance to authenticate the user.
However, if you want to alter the default configuration, you can optionally create a context for it to use instead: In this example, you set the context’s parameter to a non-zero value.
As a result, any Touch ID authentication that keychain services requires is satisfied by the most recent device unlock event, if it happened within the given number of seconds (up to five minutes).
Then use this query to actually create the item: Test the return status and handle error conditions in a way that makes sense for your app.
Without this key, the system won’t allow your app to use Face ID.
The value for this key is a string that the system presents to the user the first time your app attempts to use Face ID.
The string should clearly explain why your app needs access to this authentication mechanism.
The system doesn’t require a comparable usage description for Touch ID.
Before attempting to authenticate, test to make sure that you actually have the ability to do so by calling the method: Choose a value from the enumeration for which to test.
The name of your app already appears before the reason you give, so you don’t need to include that in your message.
When your app tries to use Touch ID, a system message asks the user to present a finger to scan.
Users don’t get a final opportunity to cancel.
Users with Touch ID (or no biometry at all) don’t need the message because they can cancel the operation before the scan actually takes place.
You test what kind of biometry the device supports by reading the context’s parameter: This parameter only contains a meaningful value after you run the method on the context at least once.
